title: Module 2 quiz: Theming and design tokens

choices.text: `extend` ADDS to Tailwind's defaults; bare `theme` REPLACES them entirely. Replacing wipes Tailwind's well-tuned base palette (`gray`, `red`, etc.) and scales. You want your brand colors alongside Tailwind's defaults, not instead. Almost always use extend.

questions.explanation: extend adds; bare theme replaces. Defaults are excellent — keep them.

questions.prompt: Need light + dark theme, with `bg-primary/50` working for tints in both. CSS variable setup?

choices.text: Space-separated RGB channels: `--primary: 59 130 246`. Reference in tailwind.config as `'rgb(var(--primary) / <alpha-value>)'`. Now `bg-primary` uses full opacity, `bg-primary/50` uses 50% opacity, `bg-primary/10` for subtle backgrounds. Without space-separated RGB, the `<alpha-value>` substitution can't inject opacity into a pre-formatted color string. Define light values in `:root` and dark values in `.dark { --primary: ...; }`; toggle the .dark class to swap themes.

questions.explanation: Space-separated RGB + rgb(var(--x) / <alpha-value>) enables alpha modifiers.

questions.prompt: Light theme flashes before dark on page load. Fix?

choices.text: Use `next-themes` (Next.js) which sets the class server-side before React hydrates — no flash. For custom: inline script in <head> that reads localStorage / `prefers-color-scheme` and adds `.dark` to documentElement synchronously BEFORE the rest of the page evaluates. Pair with `suppressHydrationWarning` on <html> to silence React's hydration warning about the class mismatch.

questions.explanation: next-themes or inline script setting class before React loads. Eliminates FOUC.

questions.prompt: When does `prose` class fit, and when does it not?

choices.text:
Use prose for rich-text content where you don't control individual elements — Markdown rendered to HTML, CMS-authored content, blog posts, documentation. The plugin styles nested `

`, headings, lists, code blocks, blockquotes, links automatically with consistent typography. NOT for UI text (buttons, form labels, navigation) — those should use Tailwind's font utilities directly (`text-lg font-semibold`). Pair `prose` with `dark:prose-invert` for dark mode; customize per-element with modifiers like `prose-headings:font-display prose-a:text-blue-600`.

questions.explanation: prose for rich-text content; not for UI text where you control elements directly.

choices.text: Write a tiny plugin: `plugin(function({ addUtilities }) { addUtilities({ '.text-shadow-md': { textShadow: '...' }, ... }) })` in tailwind.config. Now works as a real utility with all Tailwind modifiers (`hover:text-shadow-lg`, `dark:text-shadow-none`). Reference design-system values via `theme()` for consistency. Inline styles lose modifier composition; custom plugins are the right tool when you need a utility pattern across many components.

questions.explanation: Custom plugin via addUtilities. Gets full modifier composition.
